Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. <summary>
  <request>Investigate and fix Telegram bot menu command issues preventing router readiness</request>
  <investigated>Examined bot command registration flow in src/bot-setup.ts and identified menu scope pollution issue causing router initialization failures</investigated>
  <learned>Telegram bot menu commands can leave residual scope configurations (all_private_chats) that interfere with subsequent menu setups. deleteMyCommands with specific scope is idempotent and safe to call on every startup as a self-repair mechanism. Environment variable inheritance to child processes (both agent subprocesses and MCP subprocesses) can cause token leakage and 409 conflict crashes.</learned>
  <completed>Three commits shipped: (1) e60b747 - Child agents no longer inherit bot token, eliminating 409 crash loop root cause. (2) 33c261c - MCP subprocesses also isolated from token inheritance, reducing leak surface. (3) 68b13f3 - Added deleteMyCommands call before menu setup in registerBotCommands for automatic scope cleanup on startup. All changes type-checked and committed. Manual menu cleanup performed, bot menu now functioning normally.</completed>
  <next_steps>Waiting for bridge restart to apply commits 33c261c and 68b13f3 (currently running instance only has first fix). User will restart start.bat when convenient to activate all three fixes.</next_steps>
  <notes>The fix progression shows defense-in-depth: first eliminated the crash loop at the source (token inheritance), then prevented future token leakage vectors (MCP processes), finally added automatic cleanup insurance (startup scope reset). The idempotent deleteMyCommands ensures the bot self-heals even if external tools modify the menu scope.</notes>
</summary>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Embedding router readiness check mechanism</title>
  <subtitle>Doctor pattern health check validates embedder initialization and centroid computation before allowing semantic routing</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>isEmbedRouterReady() in src/embed-router.ts returns true only when embedder !== null AND centroids.size > 0</fact>
    <fact>checkEmbedRouter() in src/doctor.ts reports "router 未就緒" when isEmbedRouterReady() returns false</fact>
    <fact>Embedding router readiness is checked by 6 modules: doctor.ts, enrichment.ts, index-retrieval.ts, index.ts, intent-classifier.ts, memory-recall.ts</fact>
    <fact>Centroid computation happens during initEmbedRouter() which embeds each notebook's repr from routing.json</fact>
    <fact>initIndexRetrieval, initIntentClassifier, and initMemoryRecall all early-return if embedding router is not ready</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The embedding router uses a two-condition readiness gate to prevent semantic routing operations before initialization completes. The embedder must be non-null (model loaded) and centroids must be computed (at least one notebook vectorized). The doctor pattern health check surfaces this status as "router 未就緒" when either condition fails. Multiple subsystems depend on this check: enrichment logic guards NotebookLM routing with isEmbedRouterReady(), and init functions for index retrieval, intent classification, and memory recall all skip initialization if the embed router isn't ready. This defensive pattern prevents null-pointer crashes and empty-vector operations during startup race conditions.</narrative>

    <fact>initEmbedRouter logs "[embed-router] ready: model=MODEL_NAME, N centroids" when initialization completes</fact>
    <fact>routingTable is stored as module-level state and must contain notebooks array for centroid computation</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The embed router initialization is a two-phase process: first loading the Transformers.js feature-extraction pipeline (which downloads and initializes the embedding model), then computing a centroid vector for each notebook defined in the routing configuration. The readiness check gates on both phases completing—if the model fails to load or if no notebooks exist to vectorize, the router stays in "未就緒" (not ready) state. This prevents downstream code from calling embed() or routeByEmbedding() before the model is available. The console log provides confirmation when initialization succeeds, including the model name and centroid count, making it easy to diagnose whether init was called and whether it completed.</narrative>

content.thinking:
The user is investigating why the embedding router shows as "未就緒" (not ready). The grep results show:

1. The initEmbedRouter function is defined in src/embed-router.ts
2. It's imported and called in src/index.ts around line 1283
3. The initialization happens in a try-catch block that:
   - Reads notebooklm-routing.json
   - Checks if routing.version === 2
   - Calls await initEmbedRouter(routing)
   - Handles errors gracefully (ENOENT = file not found, or other errors)

4. The initEmbedRouter function:
   - Loads the model via pipeline("feature-extraction", MODEL_NAME)
   - Iterates through routing.notebooks
   - Embeds each notebook's repr
   - Stores centroids

5. Memory recall module has a comment "Call after initEmbedRouter() so the model is loaded."

This is a discovery about the initialization flow and error handling. The key insight is that the embedding router initialization can fail silently in three scenarios:
1. notebooklm-routing.json doesn't exist (ENOENT)
2. routing.version !== 2
3. Some other error during init (model download fails, invalid routing structure, etc.)

All three failures are caught and logged but don't crash the app - they just leave the router in "未就緒" state.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Embedding router initialization happens at startup with graceful error handling</title>
  <subtitle>initEmbedRouter is called from main startup sequence after loading notebooklm-routing.json with non-blocking failure modes</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>initEmbedRouter is called in src/index.ts at line 1283 during main startup sequence</fact>
    <fact>Initialization requires notebooklm-routing.json file with version: 2 field in config directory</fact>
    <fact>Three failure modes are caught: ENOENT (file not found), version !== 2, or other errors during pipeline loading</fact>
    <fact>All failures are logged but non-blocking—app continues with router in "未就緒" state</fact>
    <fact>initMemoryRecall and other dependent modules early-return when isEmbedRouterReady() is false</fact>
    <fact>MODEL_NAME defaults to "Xenova/bge-small-zh-v1.5" if EMBED_MODEL env var not set</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The embedding router initialization is wired into the main startup sequence in src/index.ts, running after topic rules bootstrap but before memory database initialization. The startup code reads notebooklm-routing.json from the bridge config directory, validates it has version: 2, then calls initEmbedRouter with the routing configuration. This initialization is explicitly non-blocking—three distinct failure scenarios are caught and logged without crashing: missing config file (ENOENT), outdated routing format (version !== 2), or errors during model loading. When any of these occur, the router stays in "未就緒" state and dependent subsystems (memory recall, index retrieval, intent classifier, wiki retrieval) detect this via isEmbedRouterReady() and skip their own initialization. This graceful degradation means the bot can still function without semantic routing if the embedding infrastructure isn't available.</narrative>

    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge config file resolution with fallback mechanism</title>
  <subtitle>resolveBridgeConfigPath checks MEMORY_DIR/config first, falls back to agent config directory for backward compatibility</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>resolveBridgeConfigPath checks _bridgeConfigDir (MEMORY_DIR/config) first, then falls back to agentConfigDir</fact>
    <fact>detectAgentConfigDir resolves to AGENT_CONFIG_DIR env var, or heuristically detects ~/.kiro, ~/.claude, or ~/.codex based on agent command name</fact>
    <fact>If neither primary nor fallback path exists, resolveBridgeConfigPath returns primary path for error messages</fact>
    <fact>bridgeConfigWritePath always writes to MEMORY_DIR/config and creates directory if missing</fact>
    <fact>notebooklm-routing.json is resolved via this mechanism at line 1279 in src/index.ts</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The bridge uses a two-tier config resolution strategy for backward compatibility. When looking for files like notebooklm-routing.json, it first checks the primary location (MEMORY_DIR/config), then falls back to the agent's config directory (typically ~/.kiro, ~/.claude, or ~/.codex). The agent config directory is detected heuristically by inspecting the agent command name from the config, or can be overridden via AGENT_CONFIG_DIR environment variable. If neither location has the file, the primary path is returned so error messages guide users toward the new location. This means a missing notebooklm-routing.json will trigger the "not found" log message, causing the embed router to stay in "未就緒" state. Write operations always target the primary location (MEMORY_DIR/config) and auto-create the directory.</narrative>
